Title: Title — Structured Credit Masterclass
Hook: Welcome. Over the next 90 minutes we'll walk the entire credit migration pipeline.
Talking Points: 43 slides organised in 8 acts • 4 interactive widgets and 4 checkpoint quizzes • Citations on every named example and case
Memorable Quote: You'll see how structured credit actually works — not the textbook version.
Transition: Let's start with the foundational rule that governs everything else.

Title: The Boomerang Rule (Axiom 01)
Hook: Lending is fundamentally a collections business. Cash must return.
Talking Points: Origination volume is vanity; recovery is sanity • No structuring cures structural default • 2008 NINJA loans • 2018 IL&FS • 2019 DHFL — same lesson every time • Every layer downstream depends on cash actually being collected
Memorable Quote: Cash must return. Everything else is plumbing.
Transition: Why does cash sometimes not return? Two reasons.

Title: Intent vs Ability — The Credit Faultline
Hook: Two failure modes — but only one is structurable.
Talking Points: Intent failures = behavioural (fraud willful default deception) — structure CAN'T cure • Ability failures = mathematical (cycles shocks coverage) — structure CAN cure • IL&FS and DHFL were INTENT failures dressed up as ability paperwork • Origination integrity (KYC bureau Account Aggregator) holds the intent line
Memorable Quote: Structure addresses ability; nothing addresses bad intent.
Transition: Given we can only fix ability — what's our thesis?

Title: The Core Thesis
Hook: One sentence: any credit risk is viable if structured properly.
Talking Points: There is no universally good or bad asset class • Only structures that fit (or don't fit) the cash flow • Music royalties were unbankable until Bowie Bonds • SME revolving credit was exotic until master trust mechanics existed
Memorable Quote: The work of structured credit is building containment vessels — not declining atypical risk.
Transition: Compare standard credit to structured credit.

Title: Standard vs Structured (Bicycle vs Lunar Rover)
Hook: Two completely different vehicles for completely different terrains.
Talking Points: Standard corporate credit = rigid templates (EBITDA coverage debt/equity ratios) • Doesn't fit the box → declined • Structured = lunar rover custom-engineered for volatile cash flow terrains • Cost = complexity; reward = capital access otherwise locked out
Memorable Quote: Build the rover when the bicycle won't cross the terrain.
Transition: This idea is 2500 years old.

Title: Risk Pooling — Ancient Origins
Hook: Securitisation is not a Western invention.
Talking Points: India's Srenis pooled maritime cargo risk ~8th century BCE • Hundis = early bills of exchange; Shahjog variant = early KYC restriction • Prussian Pfandbrief 1769 = first covered bond (Frederick II forced pooling of war-torn estates) • Three architectures still echo in every modern deal
Memorable Quote: What looks new on Wall Street is often ancient on the subcontinent.
Transition: Modern application — let's vote.

Title: IP Royalties Case (Interactive)
Hook: A musician wants liquidity against future streams. Decline or structure?
Talking Points: Audience votes — most groups decline initially • Reveal: Bowie Bonds 1997 $55M AAA placed with Prudential • Same blueprint: Hipgnosis Songs Fund £1Bn LSE listing (2018) • Rihanna catalog 2021; Bruce Springsteen $550M 2022; Justin Bieber $200M 2022 • Any predictable cash stream can be securitised
Memorable Quote: Decline is a structural failure of imagination.
Transition: Now let's see the architecture itself.

Title: The Credit Migration Pipeline
Hook: Every securitised deal walks five stations.
Talking Points: Box 1: Origination — digital LOS underwriting • Box 2: Pooling — asset selection diversification math • Box 3: SPV — True Sale bankruptcy-remote trust • Box 4: Tranching — Senior/Mezz/Equity stack with credit enhancements • Box 5: Investors — capital deployed • Forward flow = risk migrating outward • Return flow = the boomerang back to originator's equity
Memorable Quote: Hold the map. Every slide that follows zooms into one box.
Transition: Box 1: Origination.

Title: The Source — LOS Hygiene
Hook: Garbage in equals garbage out mathematically guaranteed.
Talking Points: Real-time GST and tax data pull • Bank statement analysis via Account Aggregator framework • Bureau review + director KYC + SARFAESI flag check • Human-in-the-loop catches what algorithms miss (circular ownership undisclosed litigation) • Aurionpro SmartLender and iCashpro are production examples
Memorable Quote: Flaws in origination compound through every downstream layer.
Transition: How do large credits get distributed in the first place?

Title: Three Structural Formats
Hook: Three architectures for distributing large credit exposures.
Talking Points: Syndicated lending = horizontal sharing among banks (tied to consortium balance sheet capacity) • Project Finance = ring-fenced single asset non-recourse SPV (highway power plant) • Securitisation = pool fragmentation → tradable institutional liabilities • Only securitisation converts thousands of ₹10L SME loans into a ₹500 Cr AAA
Memorable Quote: Securitisation is the only architecture that does true alchemy on credit.
Transition: What are the structural pillars?

Title: The Core Engineering Pillars
Hook: Three pillars: Isolate Blend Slice.
Talking Points: True Sale Isolation = legal severance from originator default • Asset Pooling = diversification math smoothing idiosyncratic volatility • Covenant Tranching = differentiated risk-reward layers from one pool • Each pillar serves a different institutional purpose
Memorable Quote: Isolate blend slice — in that order.
Transition: Now answer the central skeptic challenge.

Title: Why This Isn't Smoke & Mirrors (7 Mechanisms)
Hook: The skeptic asks: 'how can the same pool produce a better risk profile?'
Talking Points: Seven mechanisms — memorise them • (1) Bankruptcy Remoteness — True Sale severs investors from originator • (2) Eligibility Criteria — only good loans enter • (3) Waterfall — legally enforced cascade • (4) Note Classes — equity absorbs first • (5) Liquidity Facility / LOC — external standby for timing gaps • (6) Excess Spread Trap — auto-divert spread to reserve when performance dips • (7) LTV Ratchet — accelerate senior amortisation when collateral erodes • Every 'how does this improve risk?' answer is one of these seven
Memorable Quote: Always one of seven. Memorise them — they're your defence.
Transition: Now drill into Mechanism 1: legal isolation.

Title: The Legal Quarantine (Mechanism 1)
Hook: If the bank goes down do investors lose?
Talking Points: True Sale = the legal lobotomy • Originator's creditors have ZERO claim on SPV assets • SPV = orphaned trust with independent trustee • Indian backbone: SARFAESI for secured + IBC for unsecured strengthens enforcement • Risk: courts may re-characterise transfer as secured loan in weak jurisdictions
Memorable Quote: Legally severed = investor protection.
Transition: Legal sale isn't the whole story for CFOs.

Title: Risk Transfer Recognition (Accounting)
Hook: CFO question: did the assets actually leave my balance sheet?
Talking Points: Legal True Sale ≠ Accounting Derecognition (two separate tests) • IFRS 9 / Ind AS 109 = substantial-risks-and-rewards test + control test + continuing-involvement test • Most SRT and partial-transfer structures FAIL full derecognition because originator retains equity tranche • Three outcomes: Full Derecognition (rare) / Partial (common in SRT) / No Derecognition (secured-borrowing accounting) • Implication: capital relief may be partial not total
Memorable Quote: Legal isolation is necessary; accounting derecognition is the prize.
Transition: Now let's see what can be securitised.

Title: Asset Class Taxonomy
Hook: The universe of securitisable assets.
Talking Points: RMBS = granular predictable consumer-macro driven (HDFC pre-merger PTCs) • CMBS = lumpy concentrated single-default-shock risk • Auto ABS = granular short tenor residual value risk in leases • Credit Cards = revolving master trust (CRITICAL distinction: current flow vs future flow) • SME ABS = the hybrid commercial profile + consumer volume • Consumer/Personal = unsecured FICO-tiered front-loaded losses • Equipment Leasing = residual value risk (aircraft rail IT) • Distressed/NPL = ARC reconstruction at deep discount
Memorable Quote: Each class is a different terrain. Build the right rover.
Transition: What gets INTO the pool? Eligibility.

Title: Eligibility Criteria (Mechanism 2)
Hook: First line of defence. Applied before any loan sees a tranche.
Talking Points: Borrower-level: FICO/CIBIL 700+ DTI cap 40-50% employment seasoning 12+ months bureau-clean KYC+AML • Loan-level: LTV cap 75-80% current status (no delinquency) seasoning (RBI MHP 3-12 months) max loan size maturity within stated tenor • Pool-level: geographic concentration <25% any state sectoral cap single-borrower ceiling weighted-average targets replenishment criteria • Post-IL&FS RBI's 2021 Master Direction codified all of this — closed the originate-to-distribute loophole
Memorable Quote: Only good loans get in. The first defence is at the gate.
Transition: Pool behavior depends on asset type.

Title: Static vs Replenishing Pools
Hook: Pools either run off or recycle — different SPV engineering.
Talking Points: Amortizing (auto loans mortgages) — balance steadily declines • Principal collections pass through to investors paying down bonds • Revolving (credit cards LOC) — short-term assets • Revolving Period: principal recycled to buy NEW loans pool size constant • End of revolving period → controlled amortisation phase • Cannot structure a credit card portfolio like a mortgage pool
Memorable Quote: Behavior dictates engineering — not the reverse.
Transition: Now who are all the players?

Title: The Ecosystem & Structural Friction
Hook: A securitisation has many specialised counterparties — each with misaligned incentives.
Talking Points: Originator sells risk + walks with cash • Arranger structures for fees (incentive: close not perform) • Rating Agency (CRISIL ICRA in India) stamps the math — but PAID BY THE ARRANGER (structural 2008 conflict) • SPV = bankruptcy-remote trust • Trustee enforces covenants on behalf of investors • Servicer collects + handles defaults • Investors fund tranches • Each role necessary; each conflicted
Memorable Quote: Every role is necessary. Every role is conflicted.
Transition: When the pool breaks who acts?

Title: Servicers — Master/Primary vs Special/Workout
Hook: SPV is a legal ghost. Servicers do all the actual work.
Talking Points: Master/Primary Servicer = routine administration • Collects monthly payments manages escrows passes cash to SPV • The 'good weather' operator • Special/Workout Servicer = covenants breached or defaulted • Authority to restructure debt extend maturity force liquidation • In India: ARCs operate under SARFAESI for secured + IBC for unsecured • US CMBS uses 'Master/Special'; broader market often 'Primary/Special'
Memorable Quote: The SPV can't seize a factory. The servicer can.
Transition: Now let's stack the tranching tower.

Title: The Tranching Tower (Mechanism 4)
Hook: One pool → three institutional securities.
Talking Points: Senior AAA = steel vault highest priority designed to survive 99.9% stress year • Mezzanine BBB = middle priority shock absorber • Equity (First Loss) = lowest priority highest yield — originator retains as RBI MRR (5-10% skin-in-the-game) • Different yields different investors same pool • Pension funds buy Senior; hedge funds buy Equity; regional banks buy Mezzanine
Memorable Quote: Same milk. Different bottles. Different prices.
Transition: How do we make a B-rated pool produce a AAA tranche?

Title: Manufacturing AAA — Credit Enhancements (Mechanism 5)
Hook: Stack the enhancements until senior is AAA.
Talking Points: Internal mechanics: Subordination (the tower itself) + Overcollateralization (₹90 Cr debt against ₹100 Cr assets — ₹10 Cr cushion) + Excess Spread (12% yield - 7% coupon = 5% margin) + Reserve Accounts (cash trapped upfront) • External guarantees: Monoline Insurance + Corporate Guarantees (CAUTION: dilutes true sale) + Letters of Credit + Liquidity Facilities • Each layer pushes senior higher up the rating spectrum
Memorable Quote: Stack the cushions. The senior holder never feels the ground.
Transition: Static buffers absorb. Dynamic triggers re-route.

Title: Dynamic Performance Triggers (Mechanisms 6 & 7)
Hook: Structures that self-correct across the cycle.
Talking Points: Excess Spread Trap (Mech 6): when delinquencies cross threshold (e.g. 90+ DPD >5%) excess spread is TRAPPED in reserve instead of releasing to originator's equity • Originator loses carry the moment performance dips — aligned incentive • LTV Ratchet (Mech 7): when weighted-average LTV breaches threshold waterfall RATCHETS UP — more cash diverted to senior amortisation starving mezz/equity • Other dynamic triggers: Early Amortisation Events Servicer Termination Cash-Trapping
Memorable Quote: Static absorbs. Dynamic re-routes. Both required.
Transition: Let's see the waterfall mechanics.

Title: The Cash Flow Waterfall (Mechanism 3)
Hook: Like a tiered champagne tower — legally enforced.
Talking Points: Senior glass fills FIRST then mezz then equity • Insufficient cash → bottom glasses stay dry • Subordination is LEGALLY ENFORCEABLE not contractual preference • Pay equity before senior = Event of Default → special servicer takeover trustee replacement potential • Real deals have many lines: trustee fees servicer fees swap counterparty reserve top-ups
Memorable Quote: Top glass first. Always. Forever.
Transition: Try it yourself — volunteer please.

Title: Waterfall Tycoon (Live Lab)
Hook: ₹100M in. Senior owed ₹80M Mezz ₹15M Equity = residual. Distribute.
Talking Points: Invite a volunteer • DELIBERATELY try Pay Equity first → COVENANT BREACH banner • Use Reset between attempts • Correct order: Senior (80) → Mezz (15) → Equity (5 residual) • Excess spread = the residual that compensates originator for retained risk • Core principle never changes regardless of deal complexity
Memorable Quote: If you got 80-15-5 you've internalised the waterfall.
Transition: Let's size buffers with real numbers.

Title: Worked Example — Subordination Math
Hook: ₹1000 Cr SME pool. PD 3% LGD 45%. Size the AAA tranche.
Talking Points: Expected Loss = PD × LGD = 1.35% • Apply Basel ASRF at 99.9% confidence ρ = 0.04 (retail SME correlation) • Stress loss ~11.5% — roughly 8.5× EL • Equity 4.5% (₹45 Cr ~3.3× EL cushion) • Mezzanine BBB 7% (₹70 Cr absorbs UL spike) • Senior AAA 88.5% (₹885 Cr survives 1-in-1000 stress year) • This is the math regulators actually run
Memorable Quote: Senior survives a 1-in-1000 stress year. That's the AAA bar.
Transition: But what if the inputs are garbage?

Title: The NINJA Illusion + India's Equivalents
Hook: Mathematically pristine waterfall means nothing if inputs are fraudulent.
Talking Points: 2008 NINJA = No Income No Job No Assets — securitised into AAA via stated-income origination • Structures worked exactly as designed; inputs were fraudulent • India's equivalents are more visceral • IL&FS 2018: ~₹91000 Cr default cascaded through MFs PFs banks • DHFL 2019: India's first NBFC resolved under IBC (Cobrapost alleged fictitious loans) • YES Bank 2020: ~₹8415 Cr AT1 written to zero • Lesson identical: structure enhances ability cannot cure intent
Memorable Quote: Pristine paper hollow collections.
Transition: The math itself failed too — let's see how.

Title: The Correlation Trap
Hook: Li's Gaussian Copula — the formula that killed Wall Street.
Talking Points: David X. Li 2000 — workhorse formula for pricing CDOs through 2008 • Assumed default correlation static well-behaved estimated from historical data • Flaw: under severe systemic stress correlation snaps non-linearly to 1.0 • Diversification evaporates — supposedly diversified pools concentrate into single catastrophic loss • Felix Salmon 2009 Wired 'Recipe for Disaster' • India 2018 NBFC crisis showed same dynamic
Memorable Quote: When the boat tips every passenger leans the same way.
Transition: How does the regulator size capital?

Title: Basel ASRF Framework
Hook: Asymptotic Single Risk Factor — what regulators actually run.
Talking Points: Vasicek formula formalised by Gordy 2003 • Models loss distribution under one systematic risk factor • Basel II/III IRB approach uses this for regulatory capital • 99.9% confidence interval = 1-in-1000 stress year • Don't teach the math — point at it (audience trusts you more for not drilling) • RBI follows Basel broadly; specific PTC weights in Sept 2021 Master Direction
Memorable Quote: This is what the regulator runs. Trust the curve.
Transition: Now let's stress test live.

Title: War Room — Macro Shock Simulator (Live Lab)
Hook: Dial in macro inputs and watch tail loss against the capital stack.
Talking Points: Audience suggests inputs — high engagement moment • Three dials: unemployment rate shock asset correlation ρ • Structural input: equity buffer thickness • Try ρ=0.85 with unemployment at 12% — even thick equity won't save you • SENIOR BREACHED banner = the gasp moment WAIT for it • Correlation has the most dramatic effect (multiplicative term in tail loss formula)
Memorable Quote: Correlation does what no other variable does — it breaks the model multiplicatively.
Transition: Why do banks even do all of this?

Title: ALM — The Maturity Mismatch
Hook: Borrow short lend long. The structural insolvency of banking.
Talking Points: 20-year mortgages funded by demand deposits = structural duration mismatch defining commercial banking • Securitisation = ALM medicine: instantly converts illiquid long assets → liquid cash • Eliminates duration risk; frees capital to redeploy • Indian NBFCs 2018: funded long-term project loans with short-term CP • Post-IL&FS CP markets froze → couldn't roll → duration mismatch became insolvency
Memorable Quote: Securitisation is ALM medicine.
Transition: And it frees regulatory capital.

Title: Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA) Relief
Hook: ₹1000 Cr at 100% RWA forces ~₹80 Cr Tier 1 capital locked.
Talking Points: Sell senior tranche to external investors → 0% bank RWA on sold portion • Retain equity tranche at 1250% RWA but on much smaller notional • Net effect: capital freed for new lending • Economic exposure to tail risk contained in retained equity • ROE leaps • Every tier-1 bank globally runs an active securitisation programme for this exact reason
Memorable Quote: Free capital. Same yield. Same client.
Transition: Now the architectural choice: cash or synthetic?

Title: Cash vs Synthetic — The Choice
Hook: Two architectures for the same goal — risk transfer.
Talking Points: CASH (Full): assets sold to bankruptcy-remote SPV — investor cash funds purchase • Delivers FUNDING + capital relief; legal true sale + accounting derecognition possible • Workhorse for India today • SYNTHETIC: assets STAY on bank balance sheet — credit risk transferred via Credit Default Swap • Delivers CAPITAL RELIEF ONLY (no funding); workhorse for EU/US tier-1 banks via SRT • India: synthetic largely prohibited — partial economic effects via PTC + Direct Assignment
Memorable Quote: Funding or capital? That's the architectural question.
Transition: Let's see synthetic in detail.

Title: Synthetic Securitisation
Hook: Move risk without moving cash.
Talking Points: Bank keeps loans on balance sheet • Enters Credit Default Swap with counterparty: bank pays CDS premium → counterparty pays out on losses • Economically: bank sheds credit risk • Legally: assets never moved • Workhorse for European banks pre-2008 • Under current RBI rules: largely prohibited for Indian originators • Deliberate post-2008 design choice — avoid the CDS-CDO contagion dynamics
Memorable Quote: Same risk transfer no cash movement more complexity.
Transition: How does India regulate all of this?

Title: The Indian Regulatory Lens
Hook: Among the most thoughtful global regulatory architectures.
Talking Points: RBI Master Direction Sept 2021: MHP (3-12 months) MRR (5-10% retained) STC framework eligibility criteria • Synthetic securitisation + re-securitisations PROHIBITED for most regulated entities • Enforcement: SARFAESI (secured collateral seizure) + IBC (unsecured corporate resolution) + SEBI (PTC listing and trustee rules) • Post-IL&FS DHFL trustee accountability tightened materially • Built deliberately to prevent 2008 dynamics
Memorable Quote: India learned from 2008. The rulebook reflects it.
Transition: Theory meets practice.

Title: Indian Case Studies
Hook: Three institutions three eras three problems being solved.
Talking Points: HDFC PTC Programme (pre-2023): decades of monthly mortgage PTC issuances by HDFC Ltd. pre-merger • Granular pools standardised tranching deep secondary market — Indian RMBS template • IIFL/Piramal/SBI (2021-25): post-Master-Direction era SME-loan PTCs and Direct Assignments • Tighter MRR/MHP enforcement narrower eligibility — the regulatory rebirth • Edelweiss/JM Financial ARC: NPL aggregation at deep discount; Security Receipts; resolution via SARFAESI IBC settlement restructure
Memorable Quote: Same machine three institutional purposes.
Transition: How do global banks game capital optimisation?

Title: Significant Risk Transfer (SRT)
Hook: The Holy Grail for tier-1 banks today.
Talking Points: Bank doesn't need funding (has all the deposits it wants) — needs to free regulatory capital • Synthesizes mezzanine/equity portion via Credit Linked Note (CLN) • Sells to institutional investors: pension funds hedge funds sovereign wealth • Bank retains prime assets + client relationship + senior yield • Investors take tail risk for high coupon • EU + US SRT issuance crossed $25Bn in 2024 • India not yet — synthetic SRT not permitted
Memorable Quote: Maximum ROE. Minimum balance sheet.
Transition: Post-2008 global rules.

Title: The STS Framework
Hook: Post-2008 mandate to prevent another meltdown.
Talking Points: EU Regulation 2017/2402: Simple Transparent Standardised • Simple = homogeneous pools no re-securitisations (CDO-squared) • Transparent = loan-level historical data to investors no opaque NINJA collateral • Standardised = enforceable 5% retention originator must keep material economic interest • India's RBI Master Direction mirrors via STC criteria • Universal principles even where jurisdictional details differ
Memorable Quote: Simple transparent standardised — three words that rebuilt confidence.
Transition: Where is all this going?

Title: Tokenization of RWAs
Hook: Smart contracts replacing trustees.
Talking Points: Cash flow waterfall = immutable function not 500-page legal document • When SME makes payment smart contract instantly routes fractions to Senior/Mezz/Equity wallets • Live institutional protocols: Maple (institutional lending) Centrifuge (RWA collateral MakerDAO counterparty) Goldfinch (EM SME credit) Ondo Finance (tokenized US Treasuries $1B+ TVL by 2024) • India: RBI wholesale CBDC pilot Nov 2022 + retail Dec 2022 • Within a decade: trustees + paying agents = code
Memorable Quote: The 500-page indenture becomes 500 lines of code.
Transition: Take-home reference.

Talking Points: 18 terms covering the working vocabulary of any structured credit practitioner • SPV PTC DA SRT CDS/CLN OC PD/LGD/EAD EL/UL RWA ASRF MRR/MHP STC/STS RMBS/CMBS ALM LOS SARFAESI IBC RWA on-chain • Cmd+K to search across the entire deck for any term
